impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld antitrust liability and corresponding damages, finding the jury could determine the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent's scope.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors of patent holders who engage in tying arrangements have a clear pathway to antitrust recovery, promoting competitive access to unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when bundling patent licenses with exclusive purchasing requirements for unpatented products, even where those products are related to the patented technology. This limits leveraging patent portfolios in commercial dealings.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and Walker Process fraud claims for attempted monopolization.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. Tenaris had acquired prior art documents (the CYMAX Documents) before prosecuting patents on its BlueCoil product, raising questions about whether it withheld material prior art from the PTO. The court remanded for further proceedings on both patent unenforceability and antitrust liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers have a viable path to pursue both inequitable conduct defenses and Walker Process antitrust claims where evidence suggests a patentee possessed and concealed material prior art during prosecution.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire companies and their prior art face heightened scrutiny in subsequent patent prosecution. Failure to disclose acquired prior art can expose companies to both patent unenforceability and treble antitrust damages for attempted monopolization.

impacts.caseName: Seagen Inc. v. Daiichi Sankyo Company, Ltd.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an antibody-drug conjugate patent satisfied the written description and enablement requirements where the claims encompassed a broad genus of tetrapeptide linker units.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a $41+ million jury verdict in favor of Seagen, holding that the asserted ADC patent claims lacked adequate written description and enablement. The patent claimed a broad genus of antibody-drug conjugates with tetrapeptide linker units but the specification disclosed only dipeptide examples. This decision reinforces strict written description and enablement standards for broad genus claims in biotechnology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical and biotech companies defending against broad genus patent claims benefit from strengthened invalidity defenses, reducing exposure to infringement liability for independently developed products like Daiichi's Enhertu.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the pharmaceutical and biotech space face heightened risk that genus claims will be invalidated for insufficient written description and enablement, potentially undermining broad patent protection strategies for platform technologies.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-based methods for tailoring media content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming systems for tailoring content delivery to device capabilities were ineligible for patenting under § 101, reversing the jury's $2.5 million infringement verdict. The court found the claims directed to abstract ideas of adapting content to device specifications without inventive concept. This reversed the district court's denial of Netflix's § 101 challenge.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies like Netflix gain strong protection against patent claims directed to fundamental concepts of adaptive content delivery, reducing litigation exposure from patent assertion entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to content adaptation and streaming technology face continued narrowing of patent eligibility, limiting the enforceability of software and internet-related patents.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR non-institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and the Director retains ultimate authority over non-institution decisions, which are insulated from judicial review.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's retained flexibility to deny IPR institution through policy guidance, reducing the risk that their patents will be subjected to administrative challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners lose a significant challenge to PTO discretionary denial practices, meaning the PTO can continue restricting access to IPR proceedings without formal rulemaking input from stakeholders.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams state a claim for patent infringement or are properly dismissed under Rule 12(b)(6).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement complaint against Google for failure to state a claim. The patent related to web conferencing systems enabling participants to view sessions in real-time, delayed, or after completion at different playback rates. This decision reinforces the viability of early-stage dismissal of patent infringement claims where the complaint fails to adequately allege infringement.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from the availability of Rule 12(b)(6) dismissal as a cost-effective defense against insufficiently pleaded patent infringement claims, reducing litigation costs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased risk that infringement complaints will be dismissed at the pleading stage, requiring more detailed factual allegations to survive a motion to dismiss.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board correctly construed claims in a patent directed to systems for streaming partially encrypted media content, specifically the meaning of 'encryption information' in the context of adaptive bitrate streaming.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction in an IPR proceeding involving DivX's patent on streaming encrypted media content. The court adopted a different construction of the disputed claim term and vacated the Board's decision sustaining patentability, remanding for further proceedings. This case impacts the streaming technology patent landscape.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming technology companies like Netflix benefit from closer Federal Circuit scrutiny of Board claim constructions in IPR proceedings, improving chances of invalidating overbroad patent claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital media space face risk that claim constructions favorable at the Board level will be overturned on appeal, potentially leading to invalidation of patents covering encryption and streaming technologies.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a party that acquires common law trademark rights through an asset purchase agreement can establish priority over a registered mark for purposes of a cancellation counterclaim under Section 2(d) of the Lanham Act.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's registered mark 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' and dismissal of Game Plan's opposition to UNIP's applications. UNIP established priority through common law rights acquired via an asset purchase agreement that transferred both the mark and associated goodwill. Game Plan's failure to submit evidence at trial proved fatal to its claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies can strategically acquire common law trademark rights through asset purchases to establish priority over registered marks, providing a viable pathway to challenge existing registrations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants face cancellation risk from parties who acquire prior common law rights through purchase agreements, even after registration. Failure to actively prosecute opposition proceedings with evidence can result in loss of registration.

impacts.caseName: In re Bayou Grande Coffee Roasting Co.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the TTAB properly applied the doctrine of foreign equivalents to refuse registration of 'KAHWA' as generic or merely descriptive for cafés and coffee shops based on its Kashmiri green tea meaning.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the TTAB's refusal to register the mark KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops. The court found the Board's genericness and descriptiveness findings based on the Kashmiri green tea meaning of KAHWA were not supported by substantial evidence. This decision limits the PTO's ability to use the doctrine of foreign equivalents to block trademark registrations based on obscure foreign-language meanings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses seeking to register marks derived from foreign languages benefit from heightened evidentiary requirements before the PTO can refuse registration based on the doctrine of foreign equivalents, particularly for less common foreign word meanings.

impacts.caseName: Coda Development S.R.O. v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether Goodyear was entitled to judgment as a matter of law overturning a $64 million jury verdict for trade secret misappropriation related to self-inflating tire technology, and whether correction of inventorship was warranted.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed JMOL overturning a $64 million jury verdict ($2.8M compensatory, $61.2M punitive) for trade secret misappropriation of self-inflating tire technology. The court also affirmed the denial of correction of inventorship. This case addresses the evidentiary standards required to sustain trade secret misappropriation claims in the context of complex technology development.
impacts.positiveImplications: Large manufacturers benefit from robust JMOL review of trade secret verdicts, ensuring that substantial damages awards require sufficient evidentiary support for each claimed trade secret.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret claimants face heightened risk that even large jury verdicts will be overturned on JMOL, requiring precise definition and proof of misappropriation for each asserted trade secret.

impacts.caseName: Secretary of Defense v. Pratt & Whitney
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an agreement between a defense contractor and the government regarding the inclusion of 'Drag' costs in the overhead pool for cost-plus contracts was valid, and whether the Board's decision on the allocation base was final and appealable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that an agreement between Pratt & Whitney and the government regarding inclusion of 'Drag' costs in the overhead pool was invalid, reversing the Board on that issue. The court dismissed the appeal regarding the overhead cost allocation base for lack of finality. This case involves cost accounting standards governing allocation of overhead costs between government and commercial contracts for defense contractors.
impacts.positiveImplications: The government benefits from invalidation of agreements that improperly inflate the overhead pool charged to cost-plus defense contracts, potentially reducing costs across defense procurement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defense contractors face increased scrutiny of cost allocation practices, particularly regarding collaboration agreements with parts suppliers, and risk losing negotiated agreements on overhead cost inclusion.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with 19 U.S.C. §§ 1516a and 1677f, and whether this practice abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial proceedings.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the governing statute. The court held that Section 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. The ITC must evaluate confidentiality on a case-by-case basis rather than applying automatic blanket designations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers, domestic producers, and the public benefit from increased transparency in trade remedy proceedings, as the ITC can no longer automatically shield all questionnaire responses from disclosure regardless of actual confidentiality.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies participating in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk that commercially sensitive business information submitted in questionnaire responses may be disclosed, potentially requiring more careful evaluation of what information to submit.

impacts.caseName: Wonderland Switzerland AG v. Evenflo Company, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly entered a permanent injunction covering both asserted patents and whether willful infringement warranted a new trial, in a case involving car seat design patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit partially affirmed and partially reversed the district court's judgment finding Evenflo infringed Wonderland's car seat patents. The court addressed issues of literal infringement, doctrine of equivalents, permanent injunction scope, and willful infringement. The decision impacts the scope of injunctive relief available to patent holders and the standards for willful infringement in the consumer products space.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the consumer products space benefit from affirmation of infringement findings and injunctive relief, providing meaningful protection for innovative product designs.
impacts.negativeImplications: Consumer product manufacturers face infringement liability and injunctive relief for products found to infringe under the doctrine of equivalents, even where literal infringement is not established for all claims.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether OPM's regulation requiring written authorization for overtime (5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c)) is a valid exercise of rulemaking authority under the statutory requirement that the government pay for overtime 'officially ordered or approved' (5 U.S.C. § 5542(a)).
impacts.description: Sitting en banc, the Federal Circuit upheld OPM's longstanding regulation requiring written authorization for federal employee overtime as a valid exercise of delegated rulemaking authority. The court held the writing requirement is consistent with the statutory mandate to pay for overtime that is 'officially ordered or approved.' This decision impacts overtime pay claims for the entire federal workforce.
impacts.positiveImplications: The federal government as employer benefits from affirmation of the writing requirement, which provides administrative clarity and controls overtime costs across agencies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal employees face continued barriers to recovering overtime pay without written pre-authorization, even where supervisors were aware of and benefited from the additional work performed. This particularly impacts healthcare workers and others in demanding roles during emergencies.

impacts.caseName: Adnexus Inc. v. Meta Platforms, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly dismissed a patent infringement complaint against Meta under Rule 12(b)(6) for failure to adequately plead infringement of an online advertising patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the district court's dismissal of Adnexus's patent infringement complaint against Meta's Lead Ads product and remanded for further proceedings. The court found that the amended complaint, together with attached claim charts and infringement contentions, may have been sufficient to state a plausible claim for infringement. This decision clarifies pleading standards for patent infringement cases.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a more permissive approach to patent infringement pleading, with the court recognizing that detailed claim charts and infringement contentions attached to complaints can supplement allegations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies like Meta face reduced ability to obtain early dismissal of patent infringement suits at the pleading stage, potentially increasing litigation costs before reaching substantive defenses.
